summary:
The Dunhuang Mogao Caves (first cave excavated c. 366 AD, active through the 14th century) are the most important repository of Buddhist art and manuscripts in the world — a complex of 492 decorated cave temples carved into a cliff face in the Gobi Desert at Dunhuang, a key oasis on the Silk Road where travelers rested before or after crossing the desert. Over a thousand years of Buddhist patronage by merchants, rulers, and pilgrims produced some of the most extensive and best-preserved collections of Buddhist painting, sculpture, and manuscripts surviving from the ancient and medieval worlds.

The caves' importance extends far beyond art: a sealed library cave (Cave 17, sealed around 1000 AD and rediscovered in 1900 by the monk Wang Yuanlu) contained approximately 40,000–50,000 manuscripts and printed documents — the oldest printed book in the world (the Diamond Sutra, 868 AD) was found here, along with texts in Chinese, Tibetan, Sanskrit, Sogdian, Uyghur, and other languages representing the extraordinary cultural mixing of the Silk Road. British archaeologist Aurel Stein and French scholar Paul Pelliot acquired thousands of manuscripts from Wang in 1907–1908, a transaction that remains controversial in Chinese scholarship. The Dunhuang documents have revolutionized our understanding of medieval Chinese, Central Asian, and Buddhist history, providing primary sources for social, economic, and literary history that are unavailable elsewhere. Dunhuang is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of the world's great cultural treasures.
